text: Stars will always be stars, no matter what era they're playing in, but this era is absolutely fucking insane in terms of how complex and high level everything is Someone did a detailed watch of the '04 Pistons legendary defensive championship, and while they dominated their season and they earned their legacy, they pointed out that their defensive schemes would be basically elementary compared to modern schemes, and they didn't have to face the offenses of today either When people talk about X modern team vs. Y legacy team, what never gets talked about is how brutally the modern teams would scheme out the exploitable weaknesses that every legacy team had (most obvious is the amount of non-shooters that modern teams would mercilessly ignore). If it was a single game thing, I guess it's different (I would assume a modern coach would adjust quickly), but in a seven-game series? Shit would be straight up mean-spirited lol
label: analysis

text: Pacers are at 80 points, and Cavs are on 39 points (41 deficit). There's 24 minutes of the game left, so that's 60 \* 24 second possessions left. If we give Pacers half and Cavs half, that means 30 possessions left each. The NBA average is about 112 points per 100 possessions, which would be 33.6 points for 30 possessions. So \*\*IF\*\* both teams used the full 24 seconds of each possession, and if Pacers scored zero while Cavs scored 33.6 points - Cavs would still lose by 7.4 points. Obviously this doesn't factor in a lot of things, quick possessions, turnovers, foul game, Donovan Mitchell going off, etc.
label: analysis

text: He is not getting 30 million lmao, he's young but I feel Bobby Marks is missing the mark here (I'll walk myself out after this comment). He's increased his scoring by more than 8 points per game because last year he was averaging 7.3 points. Sure, you look at the raw point total and it pops, but then when you analyze his game - teams are essentially daring him to shoot as they double onto Jokic off him, so naturally he has better shooting splits. I do think 5/100 is fair for him, but 5/150 feels like an overreaction to his raw numbers which are the direct result of playing with Joker. If the Nuggets were interested in paying a starting SG that type of money, KCP would still be a Nugget. Show me a front office that'll pay Braun 30 million a year, I'll show you a front office that's probably going to get wiped by the end of that season.
label: analysis

text: I didn’t not watch it in protest. I didn’t watch it because it’s incredibly dumb Edit for the people from asking why the award show is dumb: Basically the NBA tries to make everything a spectacle. The last game of the regular season was on April 11th. That’s about the same time the voting for the awards took place. Instead of revealing it when they knew the results, they waited until after the playoffs and the draft and had the award show a few days ago. It shoehorned in celebrities and musical acts to push viewership outside of NBA fans to maximize advertising dollars. To make it worse the Coach of the year Award went to a guy who got fired after his team lost in the playoffs. He had to go up and make a speech. I get that it’s a business/financial move because advertisers will pay a ton of money but fans are mad they wait so long to reveal the results solely for the benefit of the NBA. TL;DR: Awards are good. Making them into a spectacle 2.5 months after voting is not.
label: analysis
